title: Cost Planning
description: Learn how projects estimate, budget, and control costs throughout the project lifecycle.

sections.title: Learning Objectives
items: Explain why cost planning is critical for project success.
Differentiate between estimating, budgeting, and cost control.
Explain contingency reserves and management reserves.
Recognize how earned value can monitor project performance.

sections.title: Introduction
content: Every project requires financial resources. Whether constructing a bridge, developing software, or launching a new product, organizations need to understand how much the project will cost before committing to it.
Cost planning is the process of estimating, allocating, and controlling the financial resources required to complete the project successfully.
Good cost planning increases confidence among stakeholders, supports better decision making, and reduces the likelihood of budget overruns.

sections.title: The Three Stages of Cost Planning
content: **1. Cost Estimating** predicts the expected cost of individual activities.
**2. Cost Budgeting** combines those estimates into an approved project budget.
**3. Cost Control** compares actual spending with the budget throughout project execution.

sections.title: Example: Building a Warehouse
content: Suppose a company wants to build a warehouse.
Engineers estimate material costs, labor costs, equipment rental, permits, and contingency.
These estimates are combined into a total project budget.
As construction progresses, actual invoices are compared with the budget to detect cost overruns early.

sections.title: Cost Estimating Techniques
content: **Analogous Estimating** uses historical projects.
**Parametric Estimating** uses statistical relationships such as cost per square meter.
**Bottom-Up Estimating** estimates every work package individually before summing the costs.
**Three-Point Estimating** considers optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic scenarios to account for uncertainty.

sections.title: Direct and Indirect Costs
content: Direct costs are directly linked to project work, such as materials, labor, and equipment.
Indirect costs support the project but cannot easily be assigned to one activity, such as office rent, utilities, and administrative salaries.

sections.title: Contingency vs Management Reserve
content: Projects rarely go exactly as planned.
Contingency reserves cover known risks that have been identified during planning.
Management reserves are additional funds kept by senior management for unforeseen events outside the identified project risks.

sections.title: Practical Example
content: A shipyard estimates that steel prices may fluctuate by 5%.
The project includes a contingency reserve to cover this known uncertainty.
If an unexpected government regulation later requires major redesign, management reserve—not contingency—would normally be used.

sections.title: The Cost Baseline
content: Once estimates are approved, they become the project's Cost Baseline.
The baseline represents the authorized budget used to measure project performance.
Actual costs are continuously compared against the baseline throughout execution.

sections.title: Monitoring Project Costs
content: Project managers continuously compare planned spending with actual spending.
One widely used approach is Earned Value Management (EVM), which combines scope, schedule, and cost into a single performance measurement system.
Important EVM metrics include:
• Planned Value (PV)
• Earned Value (EV)
• Actual Cost (AC)
• Cost Variance (CV = EV − AC)
• Schedule Variance (SV = EV − PV)
These indicators help managers identify problems before they become critical.

sections.title: Expert Insight
content: Many project failures are not caused by inaccurate estimates but by poor cost control. Successful organizations update forecasts regularly and act quickly when spending begins to deviate from the approved budget.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
content: Confusing estimates with approved budgets.
Ignoring indirect costs.
Using contingency reserves for unrelated scope changes.
Failing to update forecasts during execution.
Monitoring only spending instead of earned value.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
items: Cost planning includes estimating, budgeting, and cost control.
Several estimating techniques exist, each suited for different situations.
Direct and indirect costs should both be considered.
Contingency reserves manage known risks, while management reserves cover unknown events.
The Cost Baseline provides the reference for measuring project performance.
Earned Value Management helps monitor cost and schedule performance simultaneously.

sections.title: Reflection
questions: Why might two different estimating techniques produce different results for the same project?
When would contingency reserve be appropriate instead of management reserve?
Why is monitoring costs throughout the project just as important as creating the initial budget?
